issue_title: Lockout by username and IP does not work with custom username
issue_body:
`AXES_LOCK_OUT_BY_COMBINATION_USER_AND_IP` does not work if the username was modified before being sent to `authenticate`.

1. `signals.py` receives the `user_login_failed` signal, and correctly fetches the modified `username` from `credentials`.
( `username = credentials[settings.AXES_USERNAME_FORM_FIELD]` )
1. It then calls `get_user_attempts` with *only* the `request` as arguments.
1. Which leads to `_query_user_attempts` (in `attempts.py`) to *incorrectly* fetch `username` from `request` (returning the unmodified value).
( `username = request.POST.get(settings.AXES_USERNAME_FORM_FIELD, None)` )
1. 0 attempts are found because the `username` in the database is the one from `credentials`, which leads to the creation of a new attempt row with every failed login, instead of incrementing the tries and eventually locking out the user.

`username` should be passed through such that it properly queries attempts.

issue_title: Not passing a protected header when creating a compact JWE causes KeyError
issue_body:
Minimal test case:

```python
# testcase.py
from jwcrypto.jwe import JWE
from jwcrypto.jwk import JWK

payload = "some payload text"

jwe = JWE(payload)

key = JWK.generate(kty='RSA', size=4096)

header = {
    "alg": "RSA-OAEP-256",
    "enc": "A256CBC-HS512",
    "typ": "JWE",
}

jwe.add_recipient(key, header)

serialised = jwe.serialize(compact=True)
```

```
$ python testcase.py
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "testcase.py", line 18, in <module>
    serialised = jwe.serialize(compact=True)
  File "/home/michael/code/jwcrypto/jwcrypto/jwe.py", line 285, in serialize
    ph = json_decode(self.objects['protected'])
KeyError: 'protected'
```

It seems like this is a fairly easy fix to initialise the 'protected' key in 'objects' to `{}`, but I'm not sure if this is allowed by the JWE spec?

First of all thanks for an excellent library. I'm using the library for JWE de/encryption in combination with square/js-jose.

It took me a while to figure out the API and what I ended up with is:
```
def encrypt(plaintext, public_jwk)
    protected = '{"alg":"RSA-OAEP","enc":"A256GCM"}'
    jwe = JWE(plaintext=plaintext, protected=protected)
    jwe.add_recipient(public_jwk)
    ciphertext = jwe.serialize(compact=True)
    return ciphertext

def decrypt(ciphertext, private_jwk):
    jwe = JWE()
    jwe.deserialize(ciphertext, key=private_jwk)
    plaintext = jwe.payload.decode('utf-8')
    return plaintext
```
Unfortunately those two functions look very different and aren't at all symmetric. If this is at all reasonable code, I found a couple of things non-obvious:

* The protected header could use an example in the docs. Otherwise you have to read large parts of the RFC to understand what it means.
* The protected argument to JWE is a string, I'd expected it to be a Python dict and assumed the API would take care of the conversion internally.
* It would be nice if there would be a key argument to ``JWE.__init__`` adding a default recipient. Since I use compact encoding, there can only be a single recipient and this would avoid the extra call to add_recipient.
* The JWE.deserialize function notes that it destroys the entire JWE state. Wouldn't it make more sense to change this into a class or staticmethod which internally creates a new JWE instance?
* JWE.deserialize has no return value. I expected it to return the plaintext and not get it from the payload property.

This is all well meant feedback after I used the library for a single purpose, so I might very well be missing larger implications or context.

Thanks! Hanno

Currently neither Python nor Numpy implement a ​selection algorithm (http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Selection_algorithm). This means that median calculation is done by means of a sort (which is O(N log N)) instead of selection of kth percentile (which is O(N)).

There's a enhancement request in numpy to provide a selection-based median (​http://projects.scipy.org/numpy/ticket/1213). In the mean time, an implementation of median using a selection algorithm in C/C++ would be useful.

lambda executor should include stdout in exceptoin message
issue_body:
https://github.com/localstack/localstack/blob/master/localstack/services/awslambda/lambda_executors.py#L67

```python
   def run_lambda_executor(self, cmd, env_vars={}, async=False):
        process = run(cmd, async=True, stderr=subprocess.PIPE, outfile=subprocess.PIPE, env_vars=env_vars)
        if async:
            result = '{"async": "%s"}' % async
            log_output = 'Lambda executed asynchronously'
        else:
            return_code = process.wait()
            result = to_str(process.stdout.read())
            log_output = to_str(process.stderr.read())

            if return_code != 0:
                raise Exception('Lambda process returned error status code: %s. Output:\n%s' %
                    (return_code, log_output))
        return result, log_output
```

When the return code is not zero, the executor function will raise an Exception with return code and stderr information, but the stdout is missing.

When there are errors in lambda, not only the stderr, but also the stdout is necessary for analyzing, so the stdout should also be included in the Exception message.

issue_title: The trace for an error in a context with a lot of dependents is redundant
issue_body:
The `Graph.trace` for a cycle detected in a context where the failed/cyclic Node has a lot of dependents is very redundant. See @ericzundel 's response to my question on https://rbcommons.com/s/twitter/r/4087/ for an example.

To repro, introduce a failure inside of a graph, and then list a target spec that covers a bunch of things that depend on it... ie: break guava in 3rdparty, and then attempt to run `./pants list ::`.

Most likely what we want to do is invert the trace: rather than rendering downward from the roots, we should discover all failed nodes, and then, for each node, choose some number of relevant paths (or just one: the shortest...?) to the root to render.

issue_title: Distribution has an undeclared dependency on JVM

issue_title: Consider renaming the environment directory for node
issue_body:
So npm install -g from inside a git repo that contains a node_env will put the contents of the node environment into the package itself (wat!)

According to https://docs.npmjs.com/files/package.json#files we might be able to get away with starting the package with a dot and an underscore to take advantage of this behaviour to avoid the other one.

Disclaimer: An idea for a QoL thing I'm not fully convinced about the usefulness of myself.

Intro: We have several dozen repos that all use pre-commit. It can be a pain to manage the .pre-commit-config.yaml files in each of those repositories separately i.e. we may start or stop using some hooks across all repos and then it takes some effort to update all their .pre-commit-config.yaml files. I and others have various scripts and tools built to do mass updates across repositories, but I was thinking about whether there was a simpler way to maintain and think about this.

Idea: Versioned 'suites' or 'collections' of hooks.
Imagine a repo `pre_commit_hook_collections` containing some directory `collections/`:
```
collections/
  python_package_hooks.yaml
  service_hooks.yaml
  frontend_service_hooks.yaml
  ...
```
Each of those is a pre-commit config file with the same format as .pre-commit-config.yaml. python_package_hooks.yaml for example might have all the basic hooks we want in every single python package (line ending fixer, pep8, no large files, etc). When we create a new python package we would have it's .pre-commit-config.yaml file look like:
```
-   repo: git@git.example.com:pre-commit/pre-commit-hook-collections
    sha: e9446ff0cc8ffa65309e0a37c247518127a3bf18
    collections:
    -   id: python-package-hooks
        language_version: python3.6
```

`pre-commit autoupdate` would bump the version of hook collections same as it bumps version of hooks now.

Having a repo's .pre-commit-config.yaml include collection(s) does not mean it can not also specify additional regular precommit hooks. For example, if we add some yaml file to our python package we might specifically add a yaml validator or sorter hook to it. 

When we want to add/remove hooks across many repositories it then becomes a job of updating the collections repository with those changes. Then as package authors across the team or multiple teams update their hooks they'll get those changes. 

Looking for thoughts on usefulness of this idea, and feasibility.

If you've got some buggy C-level code, running in gdb can be very useful because it prints out a very detailed traceback, with full argument names and values. This could be achieved by something like `gdb -return-child-result -batch -ex r -ex bt --args python -m pytest $PYTEST_ARGS`.

However, gdb doesn't work with distributed testing because they are separate process which gdb doesn't know anything about. There is the `pytest-faulthandler` plugin which is handy but it produces a much more limited traceback with addresses only.

It would be nice if there were some way to run the child processes in gdb like this to automatically get these backtraces.

issue_title: Need a option for DNS through proxy
issue_body:
### Checklist

- [ ] This is a bug report.
- [x] This is a feature request.
- [ ] This is a plugin (improvement) request.
- [ ] I have read the contribution guidelines.

### Description
Steamlink can't resolve hostnames through the proxy server. If you receive a incorrect ip of the host via DNS spoofing, the option --http-proxy and --https-proxy may not work correctly.

### Expected / Actual behavior
Expected: Steamkink connect to youtube server through proxy and work fine.
Actual: Read timed out.

### Reproduction steps / Explicit stream URLs to test
1. Run a socks5 proxy server on port 1080
2. streamlink --hls-live-restart --http-proxy "socks5://127.0.0.1:1080" --https-proxy "socks5://127.0.0.1:1080" https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fO8x9MZ8m9g

### Logs
[cli][debug] OS:         Windows 10
[cli][debug] Python:     3.6.5
[cli][debug] Streamlink: 0.12.1
[cli][debug] Requests(2.18.4), Socks(1.6.7), Websocket(0.47.0)
[cli][info] Found matching plugin youtube for URL https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=fO8x9MZ8m9g
error: Unable to open URL: https://youtube.com/get_video_info (SOCKSHTTPSConnectionPool(host='youtube.com', port=443): Read timed out. (read timeout=20.0))

### Comments, screenshots, etc.
This is the log of my socks5 proxy when I run the step 2:
[2018-05-15 21:26:52] connect to 8.7.198.45:443

And this is the log when I visit https://youtube.com on firefox(Use the same socks5 proxy and it works fine)
[2018-05-15 21:28:51] connect to youtube.com:443

And this is the ping log to youtube.com on my pc
Pinging youtube.com [8.7.198.45] with 32 bytes of data:
Request timed out.
Request timed out.

Stramlink resolve the hostname 'youtube.com' direct and receive the incorrect ip '8.7.198.45' via DNS spoofing. Firefox works fine because it send the DNS request through the proxy.

issue_title: Type of PyArrow.Lib.Buffer for dump_dataflow_to_tfrecord w/ pyarrow
issue_body:
I am trying to dump a dataflow to both LMDB/TFRecord. While dumping LMDB is fine, but TFRecord will hit the following Error:

====TraceBack====
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "RecordIO2TFRecord.py", line 24, in <module>
    tensorpack.dataflow.dftools.dump_dataflow_to_tfrecord(ds, args.tfrecord)
  File "/home/hsienkai.kuo/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/tensorpack/dataflow/dftools.py", line 124, in dump_dataflow_to_tfrecord
    writer.write(dumps(dp))
  File "/home/hsienkai.kuo/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/tensorflow/python/lib/io/tf_record.py", line 128, in write
    self._writer.WriteRecord(record)
  File "/home/hsienkai.kuo/.local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/tensorflow/python/pywrap_tensorflow_internal.py", line 503, in WriteRecord
    return _pywrap_tensorflow_internal.PyRecordWriter_WriteRecord(self, record)
**TypeError: expected string or Unicode object, pyarrow.lib.Buffer found**

After a quick check of PyArrow, found a workaround is to add "to_pybytes()" a pyarrow.lib.buffer object (utils/serialize.py). The patch is tested w/ dump_dataflow_to_tfrecord.

```
def dumps_pyarrow(obj):     
    """
    Serialize an object.

    Returns:
        Implementation-dependent bytes-like object
    """    
    return pa.serialize(obj).to_buffer().to_pybytes()
```

Please any check/comment if the workaround is reasonable, and if it is good as a patch. Thanks

Make it possible to interact with multiple versions of the Satellite API using NailGun. Currently, NailGun targets the most up-to-date version of the Satellite API at all times. The mechanism for supporting this is unclear. Simply creating a new git branch for each version of the Satellite API is a bad idea. Many commits would have to be cherry-picked from master to other branches and numerous similar packages would need to be uploaded to PyPi, all of which is error-prone and labor-intensive. A better mechanism would be to set an element in the config file or at run-time that sets the target API version. How to support this is unclear.

Hierarchical topics are not handled correctly. More concretely, if I subscribe to a topic at a high level in the hierarchy I will also receive messages published at a lower level. 

I tested the following simple example:
1. Connect with Client C1 and receive `ConnAck` 
2. Subscribe with C1 to topic "my_topic" and receive `SubAck` 
3. Publish message `"my_message"` with C1 and QoS 1 to `"my_topic/other_topic"` and receive `PubAck` and additionally a `Publish`-message for `"my_topic/other_topic"` with message  `"my_message"`

Thus, the subscription to "my_topic" works like a subscription to "my_topic/#". I would interpret this as a violation of the MQTT-standard and I also checked with emqtt and mosquitto, which would not send a `Publish` in response to the last step.

A quick fix would be to change Line 623 from
`match_pattern = re.compile(a_filter.replace('#', '.*').replace('$', '\$').replace('+', '[/\$\s\w\d]+'))`
to
`match_pattern = re.compile("^"+a_filter.replace('#', '.*').replace('$', '\$').replace('+', '[/\$\s\w\d]+')+"$")`

However, I am neither an Python-expert nor did I test the fix thoroughly.

def run_transaction(transactor, callback):

pyscript is broken on windows when certain absolute paths are used.

This can be fixed by using a raw/repr form which adds escapes for backslashes.

issue_title: Be more explicit about how to run scripts with the autoscaler
issue_body:
The documentation should walk the user through how exactly to set up a cluster and execute a script, and especially describe what needs to be executed where, i.e. the "ray create_or_update" command is run on a laptop and the script is run on the head node.

This may seem obvious to us, but is not obvious for users and it is really important that the experience of setting up a cluster and running a script is as smooth as possible.

This can either be part of the autoscaling documentation, or be part of a tutorial "setting up a cluster".

One serious problem with long-running jobs is that the Redis servers fill up with metadata which never gets evicted. In practice, this can mean that a Ray cluster needs to be restarted periodically, which is a bit of a deal breaker.

cc @imzhenyu

issue_title: Serialization of list of all same numpy arrays blows up
issue_body:
It seems Ray does not handle serialization of an array which points to same numpy arrays well. I would hope that it would store the numpy array only once, and when reading, reconstruct the references back, to again keep only one numpy array.

The issue we are having is that we have a format where we read images and then have boundaries on image to extract subimages from. So we would like to split this into two steps, one which reads all images in (and same images can exist multiple times), and then another which extracts a needed boundary.

```python
import numpy as np
import ray
ray.init()

arr = np.zeros(100000000)

%time arr_id = ray.put(arr)
%time arr_val = ray.get(arr_id)

# same reference, out of memory

arrs = [arr] * 10000000

%time arrs_id = ray.put(arrs)
%time arrs_val = ray.get(arrs_id)

# transfer ids

issue_body:
_From [thomas.mangin](https://code.google.com/u/thomas.mangin/) on August 19, 2013 15:01:20_

Have an option to have ExaBGP save its RIB to disk as well/instead of memory
For example using sqlite3

It should be a nice feature to double click on a copy name and have the copy expandend in the source editor in read only rows.

Is it necessary to send `GET` request to S3 every time the file is uploaded? 

In methods `S3FS.setbinfile()` and `S3FS.setbytes()`, it sends two GET requests. First time, it checks if the the parent directory exists (calls `S3FS.isdir()`).  Second time, it checks if the URL is not a directory (calls `S3FS.getinfo()`).  

These operations are quite expensive. The effective upload speed increases at least x3 times when these methods are not called.

Here is the existing code:

    def setbinfile(self, path, file):
        _path = self.validatepath(path)
        _key = self._path_to_key(_path)

        if not self.isdir(dirname(path)):
            raise errors.ResourceNotFound(path)
        try:
            info = self.getinfo(path)
            if info.is_dir:
                raise errors.FileExpected(path)
        except errors.ResourceNotFound:
            pass

        with s3errors(path):
            self.client.upload_fileobj(file, self._bucket_name, _key)

Proposed solution:

    def setbinfile(self, path, file):
        _path = self.validatepath(path)
        _key = self._path_to_key(_path)

        with s3errors(path):
            self.client.upload_fileobj(file, self._bucket_name, _key)


And similar changes should be applied to `setbytes()` method.

issue_title: Client consumer taking a long time to subscribe when run alongside other consumers
issue_body:
I'm running with python 3.6.2, librdkafka 0.11.1, and confluent-kafka 0.11.0.

My application can run in one of two ways. In the first configuration, it uses two high level producers to send two types of jobs on two different topics. When those jobs are completed, messages get sent back on an additional two topics which are subscribed to by two additional high level consumers. These producers and consumers are run inside of processes created by python's multiproccessing.Process. When the consumers are created, they use
```
consumer.subscribe([topic], on_assign=reset_offsets)
```
and
```
def reset_offsets(consumer, partitions):
  self._partition_count = len(partitions)
  for p in partitions:
    current_offsets = consumer.get_watermark_offsets(p)
    p.offset = current_offsets[1]
  consumer.assign(partitions)
  status['assigned'] = True
```
to ignore old messages and to set the status flag when the consumer is subscribed. This flag is used to indicate when the producers can start sending out jobs. In the first configuration, subscription is relatively quick and happens after a few seconds.

In the second configuration, a consumer runs in the main process which listens for job requests from an API. When a request comes in, a function is called which spawns the additional consumers and producers as described above. However, in this second configuration, job subscription takes much longer (about 1 or 2 minutes), and I'm wondering why this happens. I've tried looking at the logs, but I didn't see anything useful or any glaring error messages. Are these consumers interacting with each other or sharing resources?

The consumer and producer are using SSL to connect in both application configurations, but beyond that the only optional config option that I set is 'auto.offset.reset': 'smallest'. I should be able to provide client logs if needed.

issue_title: Advice on setting up a cluster?
issue_body:
Hi,

Thanks for making your tools available. I have a use-case that's very similar to mystic: I want to do hyper-parameter searches for ML models. As well as larger search jobs, I'd like to be able to submit individual training runs onto the same cluster.

My question is, how should I structure this? The simplest solution would be for my workstation to tunnel to the cluster and submit a `ParallelPool.map()` with the single task. However, I think this will assume the client will be connected for the lifetime of the job. This seems bad.

Instead I'm thinking I want something more like how Torque/SLURM are set up: I want to submit the job as a message, and have it complete asynchronously. I tried just using `amap`, but I'm getting a zero-division error if I set 0 local nodes (obviously I don't want my workstation in the cluster). I don't know whether this particular error is just a small bug I can fix, or whether using `amap` doesn't make sense here.

Thanks,
Matt

issue_title: Django choices don't work if first character of choice is a number
issue_body:
If you have a model like this:

```
class Foo(Model):
    bar = models.IntegerField(choices=((0, "0 - zero"), (1, "1 - one")))
```

this will attempt to create an enum with name `0_ZERO`, which is invalid -- the first character has to be `[_a-zA-Z]`, and it fails with an assertion error in `graphql.utils.assert_valid_name`.

issue_title: Channel polling doesn't should check also token address along with channel identifier
issue_body:
## Problem Definition
Polling mechanism is making some erroneous assumptions when checking for the same channel. It  currently only checks for the `channel_identifier` where it should also check the `token_address` because the channel_identifier might be repeated across token networks.

This results in balance update notifications on channels that didn't actually have an update but share the same channel identifier.

Automatically open and close channels based on 
- the configuration  
- channel performance

currently the following breaks:
- valid messages from unknown nodes
- valid messages from outdated partners
- messages sent to non-existant channels

issue_title: zdaemon "reopen_transcript" command is not shown in "zdaemon -h"
defaultFactory are not handled via applyChanges in zc3.form.form.py
issue_body:
https://github.com/zopefoundation/z3c.form/blob/master/src/z3c/form/form.py#L46
tries to apply Changes on data if the have Changed, If you use a defaultFactory on a zope.schema field the value did not change, but also did not get written. This results in a side effect that if you try to resolve field value in a browser view the defaultFactory is executed again, and could provide a different result to different users. 

easy solution always write defaultFactory Fields:
change 

    if util.changedField(field.field, newValue, context=content):

to 

    if util.changedField(field.field, newValue, context=content) or field.field.defaultFactory:

Currently the "Remember me" together with the session activity tracking is broken:

E.g. you have two sessions with remember me open, then from session 1 you revoke the session 2. What will happen when the browser window with session 2 is reload is that the user is logged in via the remember me token (also when a user is logged in via remember me, the session activity tracking is not created). This should not happen since you revoked the session (when you logout, the remember me token is removed)

Now the question is if we really need the remember me feature anymore? Currently we are using permanent sessions with lifetime of 31 days. I think the remember me feature is useful if you have short session lifetime (like minutes or hours), but probably not when you have long session life times.

**Proposal 1: Remove remember me**
This will solve all problems with remember me loopholes. 

**Proposal 2: Keep remember me**
We will need to monkey patch Flask-Security and Flask-Login to make it work with the session activity tracking.

Any strong opinions on either side?
@jacquerie @jmartinm This affects you related to your recent PR in oauthclient: inveniosoftware/invenio-oauthclient#134

In order to support ES5+6, review if there are major changes in the query DSL so that queries we sent to ES needs to customized for both ES2, 5 and 6.

Some further explanation for why this is happening: https://github.com/pallets/werkzeug/issues/1077

In our case, this is only used to check for pretty-printing JSON... I think we should probably change it to what [flask decided to change it to as well](https://github.com/pallets/flask/pull/2193/files#diff-dc6f589d1b53806b6a6287c182b461b2L251), i.e. `s/request.is_xhr/current_app.debug`

The model specification for a widget (i.e., the list of model traits) is implemented at least two places: the kernel and the frontend. Because we want different frontend and kernel-side implementations to exist, the model state specification should probably not be tied to any particular package name or version. Also, I think the view should be defined by the model version it supports and the name/module. (i.e., the version should essentially be '*'). In order for that to happen, we probably need to get rid of custom messages (which are sent from the view to the kernel) and instead route all of that information through the state.

The model version should only support the major part of the version number (i.e., essentially be an integer). Any change in the actual attributes of the models (whether adding, deleting, or even changing intent of the attribute) should be a breaking change in the model specification.

issue_title: introduce new technical changelog tracking process
issue_body:
### Observed behavior

Notes from discussion at today's dev meeting:

* There are user needs for both a high-level executive summary of changes that can be skimmed, as well as a detailed technical overview with links to the code-base that can be searched and analyzed.
* We're already diligent about adding github milestones to every issue and PR, which creates a detailed and complete overview of everything that happened in a release. However, this overview is too noisy for general consumption.
* Asking developers to manually update another (more detailed) changelog would introduce too much friction to the development process, especially given that most information is already contained in the github milestone overview. 

### New process overview


Add a new github label named <kbd>changelog</kbd>. Update our PR template to include an action item like:

 * [ ] If this change results in a salient user-facing change, the PR or a related issue is tagged with the **_changelog_** label.

Before future releases, we'll go through and make sure that all items with the 'changelog' label have clear, meaningful titles. Then, from the high-level changelog, we can link to a github overview which filters by milestone + changelog label.

### Context

See initial discussion from @benjaoming in #3105

### TO-DO

* [x] Add new label, and start applying it to releases from 0.7.1 and beyond
* [x] Update PR template to include new language about use of the label
* [x] Update release process checklist to include language about cleaning up titles of labeled items

issue_title: Dumping PyMongo cursor does not serialize first item
issue_body:
We are currently trying to upgrade Marshmallow from version 1.1.0 to the latest version. As our internal tests started failing we discovered a commit that breaks serializing PyMongo cursors. The commit is 48903a31227ebcc0fd3d9e62debd4be94a0b73d1.

Changing `obj_prototype = obj[0]` to `obj_prototype = next(iter(obj))` causes the cursor position to change. When the serialization in the subsequent steps progresses the first item will not be serialized (as the cursor now points to the next item).

Workarounds:
- Converting the cursor to a list before passing it to dump(). 
- Setting update_fields = False when calling dump().

issue_title: Tag data types do not conform SAM
issue_body:
According to the [SAM specification](https://samtools.github.io/hts-specs/SAMv1.pdf), tags can be of six data types:
* `A` – Printable character
* `i` – Signed integer
* `f` – Single-precision floating number
* `Z` – Printable string, including space
* `H` – Printable string, including space
* `B` – Byte array in the Hex format

However, PySam implements a slightly different set: `Z`, `i`, `d`, `f`, `B`; i.e., it adds `d` for doubles, and does not support `H` and `A`. Especially `d` causes various issues as it is used as the [automatically deduced type for floats/doubles](https://github.com/pysam-developers/pysam/blob/bd4d8f9e2c78a3c72223da070a45699d82147c71/pysam/libcalignedsegment.pyx#L155).

I think that these two functions need to be fixed:
* https://github.com/pysam-developers/pysam/blob/bd4d8f9e2c78a3c72223da070a45699d82147c71/pysam/libcalignedsegment.pyx#L1912
* https://github.com/pysam-developers/pysam/blob/bd4d8f9e2c78a3c72223da070a45699d82147c71/pysam/libcalignedsegment.pyx#L145

issue_title: How to set verbose level in pysam
issue_body:
Hi all,
First of all, thanks for this wrapper, very useful. I am working on an application which uses pysam for random access to FASTA files. I would like to avoid printing htslib messages but I don't know now to control verbose level. I am aware that a verbose control feature was recently committed, but I didn't get how to use it ( specially for the pysam.Fastafile class)

Any help will be appreciated.

Thanks in advance.

Diego

From Michaela at SCOUT meeting!

https://github.com/django-salesforce/django-salesforce/blob/master/salesforce/backend/query.py#L147-L150

```
mod = model.__module__.split('.')
if(mod[-1] == 'models'):
    app_label = mod[-2]
elif(hasattr(model._meta, 'app_label')):
    app_label = getattr(model._meta, 'app_label')

If the user has specified `app_label` in the meta shouldn't that take precedence over the module path?

I want to use UDF in the query of BigQuery.
gcloud-python supports it?

As of the [1.9.16.1](https://pypi.python.org/packages/source/G/GoogleAppEngineMapReduce/GoogleAppEngineMapReduce-1.9.16.1.tar.gz#md5=eb657d3c4d8b492e40795db9d14394dd) release, the documentation available on [the wiki](https://github.com/GoogleCloudPlatform/appengine-mapreduce/wiki/3.4-Readers-and-Writers) is severely out of date.

Both ``BlobstoreOutputWriter`` and ``FileOutputWriter`` are no longer in the source, and none of the output writers in the source are mentioned in the documentation.

When using the example in the docs:

```
from google.cloud import bigquery
client = bigquery.Client()
dataset_id = 'my_dataset'

dataset_ref = client.dataset(dataset_id)
job_config = bigquery.LoadJobConfig()
job_config.schema = [
    bigquery.SchemaField('name', 'STRING'),
    bigquery.SchemaField('post_abbr', 'STRING')
]
job_config.skip_leading_rows = 1
# The source format defaults to CSV, so the line below is optional.
job_config.source_format = bigquery.SourceFormat.CSV
uri = 'gs://cloud-samples-data/bigquery/us-states/us-states.csv'

load_job = client.load_table_from_uri(
    uri,
    dataset_ref.table('us_states'),
    job_config=job_config)  # API request

assert load_job.job_type == 'load'

load_job.result()  # Waits for table load to complete.

assert load_job.state == 'DONE'
assert client.get_table(dataset_ref.table('us_states')).num_rows == 50
```

https://cloud.google.com/bigquery/docs/python-client-migration#copying_a_table

The line:
`job_config.source_format = bigquery.SourceFormat.CSV`

gives:
`AttributeError: 'module' object has no attribute 'SourceFormat'`

I am aware that this is set by default so this line is redundent but defaults may change in future releases the best way to avoid issues is to code it. Why if I specify the CSV it cause error? This should be a line that "does nothing" it shouldn't cause errors.

issue_labels: [{'id': 539494117, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/api:%20spanner', 'name': 'api: spanner', 'color': 'f8f8f8', 'default': False}, {'id': 401639079, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/grpc', 'name': 'grpc', 'color': 'bfdadc', 'default': False}, {'id': 544604244, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/priority:%20p1', 'name': 'priority: p1', 'color': 'ffa03e', 'default': False}, {'id': 77438580, 'url': 'https://api.github.com/repos/GoogleCloudPlatform/google-cloud-python/labels/type:%20bug', 'name': 'type: bug', 'color': 'd93f0b', 'default': False}]
issue_body:
Inserting rows turned out to be very slow (80 KB/s), so I am trying multiprocessing to parallelize the process.
I am currently using only one node in Cloud Spanner, the rows are sorted by primary key and there are ~30 batches that have a few hundred rows each.
For this I am using the following code:
```
pool = Pool(5)
pool.starmap(_process_batch, batches)


def _process_batch(instance_name, database_name, table_name, column_names, b):
    spanner_client = spanner.Client(credentials=get_credentials())
    instance = spanner_client.instance(instance_name)
    database = instance.database(database_name)
    with database.batch() as batch:
        batch.insert(table=table_name, columns=tuple(column_names), values=b)
```
The code hangs when it tries to enter the batch scope (it seems to never succeed in creating a session).
Any ideas?

issue_title: It is not possible to set GIT_PYTHON_TRACE option from python script
issue_body:
As documentation states, this option has to be set via environment. But it's impossible to set env variable from inside the program via os.environ. That simply would not work because it would be applied only to subprocesses and not to the program environment itself: http://stackoverflow.com/questions/1506010/how-to-use-export-with-python-on-linux

Why do I need that? For example, in order to implement -v option that is consistent (user should not have to set env variable in addition to specifying -v when launching my program in debug mode). 

BTW, other devs seem also to have a lot of confusion about that:
- http://academia.codeover.org/questions/34964465/gitpython-debug-logs-not-available
- https://github.com/gitpython-developers/GitPython/issues/222 

Thanks in advance

Previously when using GitPython 0.3.2c1 we had something that would point to a sub directory in a repo:

`repo = git.Repo(os.path.dirname('/path/to/repo/sub_dir'))`

Now after upgrading to GitPython 0.3.6 I am getting this error:
```
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "/path/to/repo/sub_dir/perc_version", line 685, in <module>
    main()
  File "/path/to/repo/sub_dir/perc_version", line 567, in main
    perc_lib.fail_if_outdated()
  File "/path/to/repo/sub_dir/lib.py", line 451, in fail_if_outdated
    repo = git.Repo(os.path.dirname(command_path))
  File "/usr/local/lib/python2.7/site-packages/git/repo/base.py", line 162, in __init__
    raise InvalidGitRepositoryError(epath)
git.exc.InvalidGitRepositoryError: /path/to/repo/sub_dir
```
I can fix by adding another `os.path.dirname` but should this be the behavior for `git.Repo` to need the top level of a repo or a bug?
